text: WOMEN AND NEWBORN HEALTH SERVICE King Edward Memorial Hospital DPMS Ref: 5257 All guidelines should be read in conjunction with the Disclaimer at the beginning of this manual Page 1 of 4 CLINICAL GUIDELINES SECTION B : OBSTETRICS AND MIDWIFERY GUIDELINES 1 ANTEPARTUM CARE 1.4 INFECTIONS IN PREGNANCY Date Issued: October 2001 1.4.1 Group B Streptococcal Disease Date Revised: September 2010 Section B Review Date: September 2013 Clinical Guidelines Authorised by: OGCCU King Edward Memorial Hospital Review Team: OGCCU Perth Western Australia 1.4.1 GROUP B STREPTOCOCCAL DISEASE BACKGROUND INFORMATION Group B streptococcus (GBS) has emerged as an important and frequent cause of perinatal morbidity and mortality. Colonisation of GBS is carried in the vaginal and anorectal flora in 10-30% of women, which can be intermittent, transient or persistent.1, 2 Women with known colonisation of GBS who are not treated will have a 1 in 200 chance of their neonate developing sepsis.3 Early onset disease by GBS infection is defined when it occurs with 7 days of birth, although the majority of cases present within 24 hours.4 Intrapartum prophylaxis with antibiotics administered to women at risk of transmission of GBS to the neonate can prevent early onset of neonatal sepsis and provides a cost effective measure. 3 Intrauterine infection of the fetus occurs due to ascending GBS. Fetal aspiration of infected amniotic fluid can lead to stillbirth, neonatal pneumonia, or sepsis. Neonates can become infected during the passage of birth, although the majority who are exposed become colonised on their skin or mucous membranes, but remain asymptomatic. Urinary tract infections caused by GBS complicate 2%-4% of pregnancies, and it is recommended that women be treated with intrapartum chemoprophylaxis because the neonate is at increased risk for early onset GBS infection.5 A large study of 600,000 live births conducted by the Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) found that antenatal screening for GBS (current practice at KEMH) was 50% more effective at preventing GBS disease than using a risk-based approach (utilised at KEMH when current status is unavailable). This is due to the screening method reaching more of the population who have not presented with risk factors. The study also found that the screening group cohort were more likely to receive antibiotics than the risk-based cohort.2 However, a current Cochrane review found that while prophylactic intrapartum antibiotics appears to reduce early onset GBS disease there is lack of unbiased conclusive evidence from well designed and conducted trials to recommend the use of prophylactic antibiotics. 4 Anaphylaxis resulting from GBS chemoprophylaxis is rare. GBS isolates with resistance to penicillin or ampicillin have not been observed to date, however resistance from invasive GBS isolates to clindamycin and erythromycin has increased.2 Another cited risk for the practice of antepartum screening and intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis treatment is the possibility of exposure to antibiotics in the neonatal perinatal period which may affect neonatal faecal flora which can impact on immunity development and later allergies. 6 Date Issued: October 2001 1.4.1 Group B Streptococcal Disease Date Revised: September 2010 Section B Review Date: September 2013 Clinical Guidelines Written by:/Authorised by: OGCCU King Edward Memorial Hospital Review Team: OGCCU Perth Western Australia DPMS Ref: 5257 All guidelines should be read in conjunction with the Disclaimer at the beginning of this manual Page 2 of 4 KEY POINTS 1. All antenatal women should be offered screening at 35 – 37 weeks gestation for rectovaginal GBS colonisation via a low vaginal and a rectal swab. 2. Screening results are current for 5 weeks. 5 3. GBS culture results are not available for 24 – 48 hours; therefore screening is not useful initially for women in labour with an unknown current status. 4. Adequate intrapartum chemoprophylaxis is defined as penicillin (or other appropriate antibiotic cover if allergic to penicillin) for ≥ 4 hours prior to birth. 7 5. When it is impractical or inappropriate to collect swabs for assessment of GBS colonisation, then assessment for risk factors strategies should be initiated, and chemoprophylaxis commenced as required.3 6. Intrapartum prophylactic antibiotics should be given for women with:  a positive GBS culture  symptomatic or asymptomatic GBS bacteriuria during pregnancy regardless of count.2, 3  a history of a previous neonate with GBS disease antibiotics regardless of present culture result. 2, 3 Rescreening is not required in the current pregnancy.2  an unknown culture or current status at the time of labour and the fetus is preterm (<37 weeks), or membranes are ruptured ≥ 18 hours, or if the woman has an intrapartum fever of ≥ 38º C. 2 7. Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis is not required for women:  having an elective caesarean section in the absence of ruptured membranes or labour because the risk of neonatal GBS is low1, 2, 3 Routine swab collection is not required at 35-37 weeks gestation for booked caesareans.2  who have a history of GBS colonisation in a previous pregnancy. 8. Women who are GBS positive with planned induction of labour (IOL) should commence antibiotic treatment at insertion of foleys catheter / prostaglandin gel / cervadil. 9. GBS positive women requiring IOL and whom oxytocin may present additional risk (i.e. Grand multiparty, previous caesarean section) should commence treatment five days prior to IOL with oral penicillin or clindamycin. Repeat GBS screening 48 hours prior to IOL. If the repeat swabs are negative it allows the option of performing artificial rupture of membranes only and waiting. 10. Women with penicillin allergy should have sensitivity testing requested at the time of screening cultures since 20% of GBS will be resistant to clindamycin, and 30% to erythrocmycin. 3 RISK FACTORS FOR NEONATAL GBS SEPSIS These include:  a positive culture for GBS within 5 weeks of birth  a previous neonate with GBS disease regardless of a present culture  symptomatic or asymptomatic GBS bacteriuria regardless of count during the current pregnancy  an unknown culture result, or no screening in pregnancy and any of the following are present –  the onset of premature labour < 37 weeks gestation  rupture membranes for ≥ 18 hours  an intrapartum fever of ≥ 38 degrees Celsius. Date Issued: October 2001 1.4.1 Group B Streptococcal Disease Date Revised: September 2010 Section B Review Date: September 2013 Clinical Guidelines Written by:/Authorised by: OGCCU King Edward Memorial Hospital Review Team: OGCCU Perth Western Australia DPMS Ref: 5257 All guidelines should be read in conjunction with the Disclaimer at the beginning of this manual Page 3 of 4 COLLECTING SWABS GBS SCREENING See Clinical Guidelines Section A 5.2.2 Low Vaginal, High Vaginal, Endocervical and Rectal swabs. METHOD OF SWAB COLLECTION 1. Women may collect their own GBS swabs following appropriate instruction.8 The midwife or doctor should perform swab collection when there are any language or communication difficulties. 2. For the single-swab method, the lower one third of the vagina is swabbed circumferentially with a cotton swab that is then inserted through the anal sphincter, 2cm into the rectum, and rotated 360 degrees. 1 A two swab technique can be used. INTRAPARTUM ANTIBIOTIC REGIMEN 1. Check for allergies prior to commencing antibiotics. 2. The antibiotic guidelines are recommendations solely for GBS colonisation. They do not apply to women with overt sepsis for which other antibiotics may be more appropriate. LOADING DOSE Intravenous (IV) Benzylpenicillin 1.2 gm mixed in 20mL of normal saline as a slow push.3 MAINTAINANCE DOSE IV Benzylpenicillin 600 mg every 4 hours until birth.3 WHERE THE WOMAN IS ALLERGIC TO PENICILLIN IV clindamycin 600 mg every eight hours WHERE THE WOMAN IS ALLERGIC TO BOTH PENICILLIN AND CLINDAMYCIN Consult with the microbiologist. MANAGEMENT OF GBS POSITIVE WOMEN WITH PRELABOUR RUPTURE OF MEMBRANES AT TERM See Clinical Guidelines Section B 2.8.1 Prelabour rupture of membranes at term. Known carriers of GBS with prelabour rupture of membranes at term should commence antibiotics and induction commenced within 6 hours. MANAGEMENT OF A WOMAN PRESENTING WITH PRETERM RUPTURE OF MEMBRANES (PROM) WITH UNKNOWN GBS STATUS Obtain vaginal and rectal GBS swabs for culture. Commence prophylaxis antibiotics (oral erythromycin) as per the preterm prelabour rupture of membranes guideline, KEMH Clinical Guideline Section B 2.6.1 ‘Prelabour premature rupture of membranes medical and midwifery management’ If the patient labours, switch antibiotic regime to IV penicillin as per preterm labour guideline. Date Issued: October 2001 1.4.1 Group B Streptococcal Disease Date Revised: September 2010 Section B Review Date: September 2013 Clinical Guidelines Written by:/Authorised by: OGCCU King Edward Memorial Hospital Review Team: OGCCU Perth Western Australia DPMS Ref: 5257 All guidelines should be read in conjunction with the Disclaimer at the beginning of this manual Page 4 of 4 WOMEN IN FAMILY BIRTH CENTRE AND COMMUNITY MIDWIFERY PROGRAM The midwife will:  confirm the woman has no penicillin allergy  obtain the order for antibiotics from the medical practitioner  insert an intravenous cannula and commence antibiotics as per recommendations for prophylaxis and medical practitioners orders. MANAGEMENT OF A NEWBORN AT RISK OF GBS SEPSIS See Neonatology Clinical Guidelines Section 8 - Group B Streptococcal Disease (pages 5-7) REFERENCES 1. Apgar BS, Greenberg G, Yen G.
